summary: High-risk regulatory changes impacting food seasoning/snack compliance: United States introduces mandatory declarations for nine major food allergens (including sesame), cochineal extract/carmine, FD&C Yellow No. 5, and sulfiting agents (≥10 ppm), alongside stricter allergen preventive controls (21 CFR part 117) and 'gluten-free' claim standards. Undeclared allergens, delisted color additives, and prohibited substances are not permitted. European Union requires new compliance for microbiology limits, covering foodborne bacteria/toxins (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes), specific Listeria shelf-life studies/sampling, and production process hygiene. Singapore has enhanced additive and contaminant regulations, requiring SFA safety assessment for all additives, prohibiting unpermitted additives, and explicitly stating general compliance and specific limits for contaminants, including a ban on exceeding limits and certain veterinary drug residues. Ingredient/additive declaration on labels remains critical. GCC/GSO & GCC implement new mandatory technical regulations for Halal food, general packaged food labelling, mandatory shelf-life periods, product specifications for sports supplementary foods, and new microbiological limits. Taiwan updates mandatory allergen declarations for 10 common allergens and sulphite (≥10 mg/kg residue sulphur dioxide). Codex Alimentarius introduces new contaminant limits for general contaminants requiring verification (CXS 193-1995). Thailand has new prohibitions on certain foods from production, import, or sale, requiring verification of specific lists.

changes.notes: The Codex Alimentarius Commission establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants in food or feed commodities, which are the maximum concentrations legally permitted to protect human health. The General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) is the primary reference.

changes.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
changes.notes: Food safety criteria define the acceptability of a product or a batch of food applicable to products placed on the market.

Shelf-life studies (durability studies and challenge tests) and sampling procedures for Listeria monocytogenes in food processing areas and equipment must be conducted according to technical guidance and guidelines to comply with food safety criteria in Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.
Process hygiene criteria indicate the correct functioning of the production process.
This is a mandatory technical regulation for general Halal food requirements.

changes.parameter: General labelling requirements for packaged food
changes.notes: This is a mandatory technical regulation for packaged food labels.
This is a mandatory technical regulation for mandatory shelf life periods.

changes.parameter: Product specifications for sports supplementary foods
changes.notes: This is a mandatory technical regulation for sports supplementary foods.
Verification of general requirements for Halal food is required for products claiming to be Halal.
Verification of specific labelling requirements for prepackaged foodstuffs is required as per this technical regulation.

changes.parameter: Mandatory expiration dates for food products
changes.notes: Verification of specific mandatory expiration dates for food products is required as per this technical regulation.
Verification of specific microbiological limits for foodstuffs and food ingredients is required as per this technical regulation.
Food additives must undergo safety assessment by SFA before being used in food sold in Singapore.
All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.

changes.oldLimit: Verification required using SFA's List of Permitted Food Additives or Food Additives Search tool
changes.notes: Only food additives assessed and permitted by SFA are allowed for use in food products.
Food products containing an additive that is not permitted must not be imported or sold.
Food businesses must also comply with the Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food.
If laboratory test results indicate contaminants, they must be within specified limits.
All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents (contaminants) in food.
Contaminants (incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
Import or sale of meat and food derived from meat containing diethylstibestrol, hexoestrol, or dienoestrol is prohibited.
Food products with contaminants exceeding regulatory limits must not be imported or sold.

changes.oldLimit: Must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels
changes.notes: For prepacked foods, all ingredients and additives used must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish
changes.oldLimit: Mandatory declaration if present as ingredient or food additive
changes.notes: Required if present as an ingredient or food additive.
Required if present at or above the specified limit.
Certain foods are prohibited from production, import, or sale. Specific lists of prohibited foods must be verified.
Delisted color additives in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30) are not permitted.
Substances listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) are prohibited from use in food.
The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
If a food product is found to inadvertently contain a food allergen due to cross-contact, it is considered misbranded or adulterated and subject to enforcement actions.

changes.newLimit: Must meet defined standard for gluten content
changes.notes: The FDA has a final rule defining "gluten-free" for food labeling, including compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods.

changes.parameter: Allergen preventive controls and accurate labeling
changes.notes: The FDA's "Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food" rule (21 CFR part 117) requires facilities to implement allergen preventive controls to prevent cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling.
Cochineal extract and carmine, derived from insects, must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products as allergenic substances.

The food source of all major food allergens must be identified on packaged food labels. This can be done in parentheses following the ingredient name (e.g., "lecithin (soy)") or immediately after/next to the ingredient list in a "contains" statement (e.g., "Contains wheat, milk, and soy."). Specific types of tree nuts, fish, and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels due to potential hypersensitivity reactions.
Various sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food at a concentration of ≥10 parts per million total sulfites due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals.
Rule no longer appeared in the latest extraction. Verify whether source changed or extraction missed it.

The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth conditions for permitted food additives. Specific provisions are available in the searchable online database by food additive, functional class, and food category. Users are encouraged to consult the Preamble for interpretation.

changes.newLimit: Adherence to Code of Practice required
changes.notes: A Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) exists for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices. This document provides guidance for minimizing contamination in this food category.

Food additives must be approved for use in the EU and comply with their conditions of use as listed in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008. Access to the Additives Database is required for verification.

Special guarantees regarding Salmonella prevalence apply to trade of certain live animals and products from Sweden, Finland, and Norway. Other EU countries or regions can obtain similar guarantees if their control programmes are recognized as equivalent to those approved for Sweden and Finland, in accordance with Regulations (EC) No 853/2004 (foodstuffs) and 2160/2003 (animals).

changes.newLimit: Not required by law, must be truthful and not misleading
changes.notes: Advisory statements like “may contain [allergen]” are not legally required and should only be used to address unavoidable cross-contact after good manufacturing practices have been incorporated. They must be truthful and not misleading.
